theory.intro: A quadrilateral has a line of symmetry if it can be folded so the two halves match exactly. It has rotational symmetry if it looks the same when turned about its centre.
facts: A **square** has $4$ lines of symmetry and rotational symmetry of **order $4$**.
A **rectangle** has $2$ lines of symmetry and rotational symmetry of **order $2$**.
A **rhombus** has $2$ lines of symmetry (its diagonals) and order $2$.
A general **parallelogram** has **no** lines of symmetry but rotational symmetry of order $2$.
steps: For a line of symmetry, imagine folding along the dashed line — do the halves match?
For rotational symmetry, count how many turns in one full circle give the same picture.
Match the shape to its known symmetry properties.

foundation.q: How many lines of symmetry does this square have?
How many lines of symmetry does this rectangle have?
How many lines of symmetry does this rhombus have?
Does this parallelogram have any lines of symmetry?
Is the dashed line a line of symmetry of this square?
What is the order of rotational symmetry of a square?
Is the dashed diagonal a line of symmetry of this rectangle?
What is the order of rotational symmetry of a rectangle (not a square)?
How many lines of symmetry does a square have in total?
Does a general parallelogram have rotational symmetry?

development.q: A quadrilateral has exactly $2$ lines of symmetry through its diagonals. Name it.
Match: which shape has $4$ lines of symmetry — square, rectangle or rhombus?
A rectangle's lines of symmetry pass through which points?
State the order of rotational symmetry of a rhombus.
How many lines of symmetry does this shape have?
Which has more lines of symmetry: a rectangle or a rhombus?
A shape looks the same after a turn of $90^{\circ}$. What is its order of rotational symmetry?
Draw or describe the lines of symmetry of a square.
development.a: $2$ through midpoints of opposite sides and $2$ along the diagonals

development.q: A parallelogram has order $2$ rotational symmetry and $0$ lines of symmetry. True or false?
Which quadrilaterals have rotational symmetry of order exactly $2$ (not $4$)?

mastery.q: A quadrilateral has $4$ lines of symmetry. What must it be, and what is its rotational order?
A shape has $2$ lines of symmetry that are NOT its diagonals. Name it.
A shape has $0$ lines of symmetry but order $2$ rotational symmetry. Name the most likely quadrilateral.
How many lines of symmetry in total do a square and a rhombus have together?
Explain why a rectangle's diagonals are not lines of symmetry.
mastery.a: Folding along a diagonal would map a long side onto a short side, so the halves do not match

mastery.q: Order of rotational symmetry is the number of times a shape matches itself in one full turn. Find it for a square.
A student says a rhombus has $4$ lines of symmetry like a square. Correct them.
mastery.a: A rhombus has only $2$ (its diagonals); its sides meet at non-right angles so midpoint folds do not match

mastery.q: Identify the lines of symmetry of this rhombus and state how many.
A quadrilateral has the same number of lines of symmetry as its order of rotational symmetry, and both are $2$. Give two shapes it could be.
A square is cut along one diagonal into two triangles. State the order of rotational symmetry the remaining triangle has about its own centre.
